title: E. Coli Bacterial Transformation
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"9tdgq","text":"Transformation is the process by which foreign DNA is introduced into a cell. Transformation of bacteria with plasmids is important not only for studies in bacteria but also because bacteria are used as the means for both storing and replicating plasmids. Because of this, nearly all plasmids (even those designed for mammalian cell expression) carry both a bacterial origin of replication and an antibiotic resistance gene for use as a selectable marker in bacteria.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Take competent cells out of -80°C and thaw on ice (approximately 20-30 mins)
Remove agar plates (containing the appropriate antibiotic) from storage at 4°C and let warm up in 37°C incubator.
Mix 2 µL of DNA (usually 10 pg - 100 ng) into 50 µL of competent cells in a microcentrifuge or falcon tube. GENTLY mix by flicking the bottom of the tube with your finger a few times.
Incubate the competent cell/DNA mixture on ice for 00:30:00
Heat shock each transformation tube by placing the bottom of the tube into a 42°C water bath for 00:00:45 45 secs is usually ideal for E. coli however 30 - 60 is the range
Put the tubes back on ice for 00:05:00
Add 500 μl LB or SOC media (without antibiotic) to the bacteria Make sure the media has no antibiotics.

Grow while shaking in 37°C shaking incubator for 1-2 hrs. 01:00:00 This outgrowth step allows the bacteria time to generate the antibiotic resistance proteins encoded in the plasmid backbone so that they will be able to grow once plated on the antibiotic containing agar plate.
Plate some 150 µL and 300 µL of the transformation onto two LB agar plates containing the appropriate antibiotic. Plate under flames to maintain sterility
creator: Tamra Mbeuh Lahom Lot
